author: John Chrysostom
book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

ccel.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

title: Matthew III. 13.

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Then cometh Jesus from Galilee to Jordan,” etc.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: With the servants the Lord, with the criminals the Judge, cometh to be baptized.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But be not thou troubled; for in these humiliations His exaltation doth most shine forth.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For He who vouchsafed to be borne so long in a Virgin’s womb, and to come forth thence with our nature, and to be smitten with rods, and crucified, and to suffer all the rest which He suffered;—why marvellest thou if He vouchsafed also to be baptized, and to come with the rest to His servant.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the amazement lay in that one thing, that being God, He would be made Man; but the rest after this all follows in course of reason.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this cause, let me add, John also by way of anticipation said all that he had said before, that he “was not worthy to unloose the latchet of His shoe;” and all the rest, as for instance, that He is Judge, and rewards every man according to his desert, and that He will bestow His Spirit abundantly on all; in order that when thou shouldest see Him coming to the baptism, thou mightest not suspect anything mean.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore he forbids Him, even when He was come, saying,

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “I have need to be baptized of Thee, and comest Thou to me.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For, because the baptism was “of repentance,” and led men to accuse themselves for their offenses, lest any one should suppose that He too “cometh to Jordan” in this sort of mind, John sets it right beforehand, by calling Him both Lamb, and Redeemer from all the sin that is in the world.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since He that was able to take away the sins of the whole race of men, much more was He Himself without sin.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this cause then he said not, “Behold, He that is without sin,” but what was much more, He “that beareth the sin of the world,” in order that together with this truth thou mightest receive that other with all assurance, and having received it mightest perceive, that in the conduct of some further economy He cometh to the baptism.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore also he said to Him when He came, “I have need to be baptized of Thee, and comest Thou to me?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And he said not, “And art Thou baptized of me?” nay, for this he feared to say: but what?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “And comest Thou to me?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then doth Christ?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What He did afterwards with respect to Peter, this did He then also.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For so he too would have forbidden Him to wash his feet, but when he had heard, “What I do thou knowest not now, but thou shalt know hereafter,” and “thou hast no part with me,”he speedily withdrew from his determination, and went over to the contrary.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this man again in like manner, when he had heard, “Suffer it to be so now, for thus it becometh us to fulfill all righteousness,”straightway obeyed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For they were not unduly contentious, but they manifested both love and obedience, and made it their study to be ruled by their Lord in all things.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And mark how He urges him on that very ground which chiefly caused him to look doubtfully on what was taking place; in that He did not say, “thus it is just,” but “thus it becometh.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For, inasmuch as the point unworthy of Him was in his mind chiefly this, His being baptized by His servant, He stated this rather than anything else, which is directly opposed to that impression: as though He had said, “Is it not as unbecoming that thou avoidest and forbiddest this? nay, for this self-same cause I bid thee suffer it, that it is becoming, and that in the highest degree.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And He did not merely say, “suffer,” but He added, “now.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For it will not be so forever,” saith He, “but thou shalt see me such as thou desirest; for the present, however, endure this.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Next He shows also how this “becometh” Him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: How then doth it so?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “In that we fulfill the whole law;” and to express this He said, “all righteousness.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For righteousness is the fulfilling of the commandments.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Since then we have performed all the rest of the commandments,” saith He, “and this alone remains, it also must be added: because I am come to do away the curse that is appointed for the transgression of the law.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: I must therefore first fulfill it all, and having delivered you from its condemnation, in this way bring it to an end.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: It becometh me therefore to fulfill the whole law, by the same rule that it becometh me to do away the curse that is written against you in the law: this being the very purpose of my assuming flesh, and coming hither.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Then he suffereth Him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And Jesus, when He was baptized, went up straightway out of the water; and, lo, the heavens were opened unto Him, and he saw the Spirit of God descending like a dove, and lighting upon Him.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For inasmuch as many supposed that John was greater than He, because John had been brought up all his time in the wilderness, and was son of a chief priest, and was clothed with such raiment, and was calling all men unto his baptism, and had been born of a barren mother; while Jesus, first of all, was of a damsel of ordinary rank (for the virgin birth was not yet manifest to all); and besides, He had been brought up in an house, and held converse with all men, and wore this common raiment; they suspected Him to be less than John, knowing as yet nothing of those secret things;—and it fell out moreover that He was baptized of John, which thing added support to this surmise, even if none of those mentioned before had existed; for it would come into their mind that this man was one of the many (for were He not one of the many, He would not have come with the many to the baptism), but that John was greater than He and far more admirable:—in order therefore that this opinion might not prevail with the multitude, the very heavens are opened, when He is baptized, and the Spirit comes down, and a voice with the Spirit, proclaiming the dignity of the Only Begotten.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For since the voice that said, “This is my beloved Son,” would seem to the multitude rather to belong to John, for It added not, “This that is baptized,” but simply This, and every hearer would conceive it to be said concerning the baptizer, rather than the baptized, partly on account of the Baptist’s own dignity, partly for all that hath been mentioned; the Spirit came in form of a dove, drawing the voice towards Jesus, and making it evident to all, that This was not spoken of John that baptized, but of Jesus who was baptized.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And how was it, one may say, that they did not believe, when these things came to pass?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because in the days of Moses also many wonderful works were done, albeit not such as these; and after all those, the voices, and the trumpets, and the lightnings, they both forged a calf, and “were joined unto Baal-peor.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And those very persons too, who were present at the time, and saw Lazarus arise, so far from believing in Him, who had wrought these things, repeatedly attempted even to slay Him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now if seeing before their eyes one rise from the dead, they were so wicked, why marvel at their not receiving a voice wafted from above?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since when a soul is uncandid and perverse, and possessed by the disease of envy, it yields to none of these things; even as when it is candid it receives all with faith, and hath no great need of these.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Speak not therefore thus, “They believed not,” but rather inquire, “Did not all things take place which ought to have made them believe?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For by the prophet also God frames this kind of defense of His own ways in general.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: That is, the Jews being on the point of ruin, and of being given over to extreme punishment; lest any from their wickedness should calumniate His providence, He saith, “What ought I to have done to this vineyard, that I have not done?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Just so here likewise do thou reflect; “what ought to have been done, and was not done?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And indeed whensoever arguments arise on God’s Providence, do thou make use of this kind of defense, against those who from the wickedness of the many try to raise a prejudice against it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: See, for instance, what astonishing things are done, preludes of those which were to come; for it is no more paradise, but Heaven that is opened.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But let our argument with the Jews stand over unto some other time; for the present, God working with us, we would direct our discourse to what is immediately before us.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “And Jesus, when He was baptized, went up straightway out of the water; and lo! the heavens were opened unto Him.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore were the heavens opened?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: To inform thee that at thy baptism also this is done, God calling thee to thy country on high, and persuading thee to have nothing to do with earth.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if thou see not, yet never doubt it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For so evermore at the beginnings of all wonderful and spiritual transactions, sensible visions appear, and such-like signs, for the sake of them that are somewhat dull in disposition, and who have need of outward sight, and who cannot at all conceive an incorporeal nature, but are excited only by the things that are seen: that so, though afterward no such thing occur, what hath been declared by them once for all at the first may be received by thy faith.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For in the case of the apostles too, there was a “sound of a mighty wind,”and visions of fiery tongues appeared, but not for the apostles’ sake, but because of the Jews who were then present.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nevertheless, even though no sensible signs take place, we receive the things that have been once manifested by them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since the dove itself at that time therefore appeared, that as in place of a finger (so to say) it might point out to them that were present, and to John, the Son of God.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Not however merely on this account, but to teach thee also, that upon thee no less at thy baptism the Spirit comes.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But since then we have no need of sensible vision, faith sufficing instead of all.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For signs are “not for them that believe, but for them that believe not.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But why in the fashion of a dove?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Gentle is that creature, and pure.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Forasmuch then as the Spirit too is “a Spirit of meekness,” He therefore appears in this sort.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And besides, He is reminding us of an ancient history.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For so, when once a common shipwreck had overtaken the whole world, and our race was in danger of perishing, this creature appeared, and indicated the deliverance from the tempest, and bearing an olive branch,published the good tidings of the common calm of the whole world; all which was a type of the things to come.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For in fact the condition of men was then much worse, and they deserved a much sorer punishment.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: To prevent thy despairing, therefore, He reminds thee of that history.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because then also, when things were desperate, there was a sort of deliverance and reformation; but then by punishment, now, on the contrary, by grace and an unspeakable gift.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore the dove also appears, not bearing an olive branch, but pointing out to us our Deliverer from all evils, and suggesting the gracious hopes.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For not from out of an ark doth she lead one man only, but the whole world she leads up into heaven at her appearing, and instead of a branch of peace from an olive, she conveys the adoption to all the world’s offspring in common.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Reflect now on the greatness of the gift, and do not account His dignity the less for His appearing in such a likeness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For I actually hear some saying,that “such as is the difference between a man and a dove, so great is that between Christ and the Spirit: since the one appeared in our nature, the other in the likeness of a dove.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What must we say then to these things?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: That the Son of God did indeed take upon Him the nature of man, but the Spirit took not on Him the nature of a dove.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore the evangelist also said not, “in the nature of a dove,” but “in the form of a dove.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Accordingly, never after did He so much as appear in this fashion, but at that moment only.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if on this account thou affirmest His dignity to be less, the cherubim too will be made out by this reasoning much His superior, even as much so as an eagle is to a dove: because they too were figured into that visible shape.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And the angels too superior again, for they no less have many times appeared in the fashion of men.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But these things are not so, indeed they are not.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the truth of an economy is one thing, and the condescension of a temporary vision another.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Do not now, I pray thee, become unthankful towards thy Benefactor nor with the very contrariesrequite Him that hath bestowed on thee the fountain of blessedness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For where adoption is vouchsafed, there is also the removing of evils, and the giving of all good things.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: On this very account the Jewish baptism ceases, and ours takes its beginning.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what was done with regard to the Passover, the same ensues in the baptism also.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as in that case too, He acting with a view to both, brought the one to an end, but to the other He gave a beginning: so here, having fulfilled the Jewish baptism, He at the same time opens also the doors of that of the Church; as on one table then, so in one river now, He had both sketched out the shadow, and now adds the truth.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this baptism alone hath the grace of the Spirit, but that of John was destitute of this gift.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this very cause in the case of the others that were baptized no such thing came to pass, but only in the instance of Him who was to hand onthis; in order that, besides what we have said, thou mightest learn this also, that not the purity of the baptizer, but the power of the baptized, had this effect.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Not until then, assuredly, were either the heavens opened, nor did the Spirit make His approach.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because henceforth He leads us away from the old to the new polity, both opening to us the gates on high, and sending down His Spirit from thence to call us to our country there; and not merely to call us, but also with the greatest mark of dignity.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For He hath not made us angels and archangels, but He hath caused us to become “sons of God,” and “beloved,” and so He draws us on towards that portion of ours.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Having then all this in thy mind, do thou show forth a life worthy of the love of Him who calls thee, and of thy citizenship in that world, and of the honor that is given thee.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Crucified as thou art to the world, and having crucified it to thyself, show thyself with all strictness a citizen of the city of the heavens.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And do not, because thy body is not translated unto heaven, suppose that thou hast anything to do with the earth; for thou hast thy Head abiding above.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea with this very purpose the Lord, having first come here and having brought His angels, did then, taking thee with Him, depart thither; that even before thy going up to that place, thou mightest understand that it is possible for thee to inhabit earth as it were heaven.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us then keep watch over that noble birth, which we received from the beginning; and let us every day seek more and more the palaces there, and account all that is here to be a shadow and a dream.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For so, had any king among those on earth, finding thee poor and a beggar, made thee suddenly his son, never wouldest thou have thought upon thy cottage, and thy cottage’s mean appointments.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yet surely in that case the difference is not much.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Do not then either in this case take account of any of the former things, for thou art called unto much greater.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For both He who calls is the Lord of the angels, and the good things that are given surpass all both word and thought.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since not from earth to earth doth He remove thee, as the king doth, but from earth to heaven, and from a mortal nature to an immortal, and to glory unspeakable, then only possible to be properly manifested, when we shall actually enjoy it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now then, having to partake of such blessings, do I see thee minding money, and clinging to the pomp which is here?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And dost thou not esteem all that is seen to be more vile than beggars rags?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And how wilt thou appear worthy of this honor?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what excuse wilt thou have to plead? or rather, what punishment wilt thou not have to suffer, who after so great a gift art running to thy former vomit?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For no longer art thou punished merely as a man, but as a son of God that hath sinned; and the greatness of thy honor becomes a mean of bringing a sorer punishment on thee.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since we too punish not equally slaves that do wrong, and sons committing the same offense; and most of all when they have received some great kindness from us.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if he who had paradise for his portion, for one disobedience underwent such dreadful things after his honor; we, who have received Heaven, and are become joint heirs with the Only Begotten, what excuse shall we have, for running to the serpent after the dove?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For it will be no longer, “Dust thou art, and unto dust shalt thou return,”and thou “tillest the ground,”and those former words, that will be said to us;but what is far more grievous than these, the “outer darkness,”the bonds that may not be burst, the venomous worm, the “gnashing of teeth;” and this with great reason.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For he that is not made better even by so great a benefit, would justly suffer the most extreme, and a yet more grievous punishment.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Elias once opened and shut Heaven, but that was to bring down rain, and restrain it; whereas to thee the heaven is not so opened, but in order for thee to ascend thither; and what is yet more, not to ascend only, but to lead up others also, if thou wilt; such great confidence and power hath He bestowed on thee in all that is His.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Forasmuch then as our house is there, there let us store up all, and leave nothing here, lest we lose it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For here, though thou put a lock on it, and doors, and bars, and set thousands of servants to watch it; though thou get the better of all the crafty ones, though thou escape the eyes of the envious, the worms, the wasting that comes of time; which is impossible;—death at any rate thou wilt never escape, but wilt be deprived of all those things in one moment of time; and not deprived of them only, but wilt have to transfer them into the hands often of thy very enemies.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Whereas if thou wouldest transfer them into that house, thou wilt be far above all.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For there is no need to apply either key, or doors, or bars; such is the virtueof that city, so inviolable is this place, and by nature inaccessible to corruption and all wickedness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: How then is it not of the utmost folly, where destruction and waste is the lot of all that is stored, there to heap up all, but where things abide untouched and increase, there not to lay up even the least portion; and this, when we are to live there forever?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this cause the very heathensdisbelieve the things that we say, since our doings, not our sayings, are the demonstration which they are willing to receive from us; and when they see us building ourselves fine houses, and laying out gardens and baths, and buying fields, they are not willing to believe that we are preparing for another sort of residence away from our city.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For if this were so,” say they, “they would turn to money all they have here, and lay them up beforehand there;” and this they divine from the things that are done in this world.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For so we see those who are very rich getting themselves houses and fields and all the rest, chiefly in those cities in which they are to stay.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But we do the contrary; and with all earnest zeal we get possession of the earth, which we are soon after to leave; giving up not money only, but even our very blood for a few acres and tenements: while for the purchase of Heaven we do not endure to give even what is beyond our wants, and this though we are to purchase it at a small price, and to possess it forever, provided we had once purchased it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore I say we shall suffer the utmost punishment, departing thither naked and poor; or rather it will not be for our own poverty that we shall undergo these irremediable calamities, but also for our making others to be such as ourselves.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For when heathens see them that have partaken of so great mysteries earnest about these matters, much more will they cling themselves to the things heaping much fire upon our head.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For when we, who ought to teach them to despise all things that appear, do ourselves most of all urge them to the lust of these things; when shall it be possible for us to be saved, having to give account for the perdition of others?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Hearest thou not Christ say, that He left us to be for salt and for lights in this world, in order that we may both brace upthose that are melting in luxury, and enlighten them that are darkened by the care of wealth?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: When therefore we even cast them into more thorough darkness, and make them more dissolute, what hope shall we have of salvation?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: There is none at all; but wailing and gnashing our teeth, and bound hand and foot, we shall depart into the fire of hell, after being full well worn down by the cares of riches.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Considering then all these things, let us loose the bands of such deceit, that we may not at all fall into those things which deliver us over to the unquenchable fire.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For he that is a slave to money, the chains both here and there will have him continually liable to them; but he that is rid of this desire will attain to freedom from both.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Unto which that we also may attain, let us break in pieces the grievous yoke of avarice, and make ourselves wings toward Heaven; by the grace and love towards man of our Lord Jesus Christ, to whom be glory and might forever and ever.
